The live loop only works with 'local' (htw serve running) or 'custom' (host-wired). Note: the skill docs name the custom path 'hermes' while the engine enum is 'custom'.

feature: htw init — write canonical unified config
user_story: As an agent setting up a new repo, I want a single command that writes a complete, schema-valid config so the repo is immediately ready for htw commands and future staleness checks.
expected_behavior: Writes a starter to {root}/.agents/skill-config/workflow/config.json with all required keys: engineVersion (stamped from package.json), brandName (basename of root), doc.* (all 8 keys), answerGate.{base,mode}, canonicalUrlBase=null, devUrlBase='http://127.0.0.1:8765', serve.{command,port}. Creates the dir with mkdirSync({recursive:true}). Exits 0 with a stdout summary. If the file exists and --force is absent, exits 1 with a stderr error + hint. The starter is the generic public shape — no host-private values. [code: src/commands/init.mjs:88-169]
hermes_relevance: Hermes runs `htw init` as the first onboarding step. The written config is the canonical highest-precedence location. Hermes can then edit specific keys via JSON patch rather than re-running init.

user_story: As an agent or CI system, I want the config to record which engine version wrote it so drift detection works without querying npm.
expected_behavior: engineVersion() reads version from {PACKAGE_ROOT}/package.json and is always written LAST (overriding any legacy-migrated value), so the stamp is always THIS running engine's version. `htw check` later compares this stamp to the running engine to detect drift. [code: src/commands/init.mjs:78-81,160]
hermes_relevance: With `npx how-to-work@latest init`, the stamped engineVersion equals the latest published version. `htw check` afterward (same installed version) exits 0. A non-zero check exit signals running `htw init --migrate --force`.

user_story: As a repo owner upgrading from the old split layout, I want one command that reads all legacy config files, folds them into the new unified shape, and writes the canonical file.
expected_behavior: Probes three LEGACY_PATHS (lowest-to-highest precedence): .agents/skill-config/doc/config.json, .claude/skill-config/doc/config.json, .claude/skill-config/workflow/config.json. For each found split-doc config (path contains 'skill-config/doc' and no top-level 'doc' key), foldSplitDoc() moves DOC_KEYS (overlayDir, themeFile, catalogPath, sourcesDir, prdsDir, plansDir, docsIndexRoute, stagesPath) under a 'doc' sub-object, keeping other keys top-level. Each folded config is deep-merged onto the starter and announced on stdout (`htw init --migrate: folded <path>`). engineVersion restamped last. No legacy found => advisory + fresh starter (never a crash). [code: src/commands/init.mjs:26-59,141-158]
hermes_relevance: Hermes runs `htw init --migrate --force` to upgrade a repo's config atomically; each folded file is auditable in the run log. Safe to run even with no legacy files (falls back to fresh starter).

user_story: As an agent re-stamping an already-initialized repo after an engine upgrade, I want to overwrite the existing config without manual deletion.
expected_behavior: Without --force, if {root}/.agents/skill-config/workflow/config.json exists, init exits 1 with a stderr error including 'Re-run with --force'. With --force the file is overwritten unconditionally. --force and --migrate compose: `--migrate --force` folds legacy configs into a fresh starter and overwrites. [code: src/commands/init.mjs:131-137]
hermes_relevance: Essential for unattended upgrades. Without --force, init exits 1 on an existing config — an expected guard, not an error, if Hermes has not asked for an overwrite. Hermes passes --force when re-running init as part of an upgrade after a check exit-1.

user_story: As an agent running htw check on an unconfigured or legacy repo, I want a clear exit code and message telling me exactly what to do next.
expected_behavior: (A) no unified config AND no legacy split config => exit 0 with stdout advisory 'no repo config found; the engine will use bundled defaults' + a hint to run init (non-fatal: bundled defaults are always in-schema). (B) a legacy split config found (LEGACY_SPLIT_PATHS) but no unified config => exit 1 with a stderr error 'found a legacy split config ... but no unified config' + the migrate command. [code: src/commands/check.mjs:177-196]
hermes_relevance: Exit 0 = OK to proceed even with no config (bundled defaults apply). Exit 1 = stop and fix before other htw commands. Case B's stderr contains the exact repair command Hermes runs.

feature: htw check — version axis (stamped vs running)
user_story: As an agent that may run on a repo configured by a different engine version, I want check to tell me if there's a version mismatch and what to do.
expected_behavior: Reads config.engineVersion and compares to the running engine via semver (ignoring prerelease/build). Three outcomes: (1) match — no problem; (2) stamp < running — 'config is behind', fix `npx <name>@latest init --migrate --force`; (3) stamp > running — 'engine is behind', fix `npm install -g <name>@latest` or pin to stamped. All version problems exit 1 with the fix command to stderr. [code: src/commands/check.mjs:93-107,202-216]
hermes_relevance: Hermes runs check before any substantive workflow; a non-zero exit is a hard blocker. Fix commands are machine-readable (one per line after 'fix:'). For `npx ...@latest` consumers the running version IS latest, so this axis is a no-op unless the config is improperly stamped.

user_story: As an agent or CI system, I want check to verify that every key the engine reads is present so a partially-migrated or hand-edited config never silently breaks rendering.
expected_behavior: validateSchema() checks: top-level keys (engineVersion, doc, answerGate); all 8 doc.* sub-keys (overlayDir, themeFile, catalogPath, sourcesDir, prdsDir, plansDir, docsIndexRoute, stagesPath); answerGate.{base,mode}; answerGate.mode in ['none','local','custom']. Optional keys (canonicalUrlBase, devUrlBase, serve, agent-guidance fields) are NOT in the schema and never fail. Each missing key/invalid value is a problem string; any schema problem exits 1 and appends the `init --migrate --force` fix. [code: src/commands/check.mjs:45-59,125-153]
hermes_relevance: A 0-exit check means 'config fully valid for this engine version'. Schema failures are deterministic and fixed by re-running init — Hermes need not interpret which keys are missing; the fix command is universal.

user_story: As an agent in CI or verifying currency, I want to optionally compare the running version against the latest npm release without making it a hard failure.
expected_behavior: With --online, latestPublishedVersion() runs `npm view <name> version` (spawnSync, 15s timeout). If the running engine is behind the published version, a non-fatal warning is written to stdout ('a newer engine is published: <latest> (running <running>) — npm i -g <name>@latest'). Never increments the problems list, never affects exit code. Network failure / npm absence is silently swallowed. Without --online the npm check is skipped entirely. [code: src/commands/check.mjs:155-163,225-229]
hermes_relevance: Hermes does NOT use --online in tight loops (15s blocking npm call). Reserve for scheduled maintenance. The advisory is on stdout — scan for 'a newer engine is published' to detect it. Omit --online in offline/air-gapped environments.

user_story: As a repo maintainer, I want check to tell me about leftover workflow-kit files (prd.html, base.css, theme.tokens.css, verify.py) no longer read by the engine so I can clean them up.
expected_behavior: Scans seven DEAD_ASSET_PATHS under .agents/ and .claude/ for known stale files (prd.html, base.css, theme.tokens.css, verify.py). Each present file produces a stdout warning 'htw check: warning: dead workflow-kit asset present: <path> (unwired — safe to delete)'. Always non-fatal (never contributes to exit 1); printed even when the overall check exits 0. [code: src/commands/check.mjs:61-70,171-175,233]
hermes_relevance: Hermes does not block on dead-asset warnings (stdout, 'warning:' prefix, distinguishable from problem lines). For a full repo cleanup, Hermes can scan for these lines and delete the listed files.

user_story: As a repo author, I want to set the default port `htw serve` listens on in config so the team does not pass --port on every invocation.
expected_behavior: config.serve.port is an optional integer; if present, `htw serve` uses it as the default when --port is not passed; absent => default 8765. The --port CLI flag always wins. The htw init starter writes serve.port=8765 and serve.command='npm run docs:serve'. [code: src/commands/serve.mjs:93; src/commands/init.mjs:108-109]
hermes_relevance: Hermes reads config.serve.port before constructing the grill-ask base URL or devUrlBase. If the repo's serve port differs from 8765, Hermes must set devUrlBase to 'http://127.0.0.1:{port}' so relative answerGate.base URLs resolve correctly.

feature: canonicalUrlBase and devUrlBase — optional URL context keys
user_story: As a repo author, I want to declare a public canonical URL (links in published docs) and a local dev URL (answer-gate resolution during authoring) so agents and the CLI use the right host.
expected_behavior: canonicalUrlBase and devUrlBase are top-level optional strings written by htw init (null and 'http://127.0.0.1:8765'). NOT in the schema validated by check — absence never fails. devUrlBase is read by grill.mjs: when answerGate.base is relative, grill prepends devUrlBase to form an absolute URL. canonicalUrlBase is inert in the current engine (agent-guidance / future use). [code: src/commands/init.mjs:102-103; src/commands/grill.mjs:68]
hermes_relevance: Hermes sets devUrlBase to the actual address where `htw serve --answer-gate` runs before relying on a relative answerGate.base. A non-default port (serve.port=9000) requires updating devUrlBase to match or grill ask polls the wrong address.

user_story: As an agent invoking any skill, I need the engine to read the repo's merged config before touching any path, port, brand, or command, so my invocations are always repo-aware and never hardcode stale defaults.
expected_behavior: Before scaffolding/grilling/rendering/serving/gating, probe config in precedence (.agents/skill-config/workflow > .claude/skill-config/workflow > .agents|.claude/skill-config/doc > bundled config/defaults.json). Run `npx how-to-work@latest check`; if non-zero, run the printed init / init --migrate fix before proceeding. Never hardcode brand, host, paths, ports, or commands. [code: how-we-work/SKILL.md:12-25; scope/SKILL.md:17-24; grill/SKILL.md:18-27; doc/SKILL.md:18-31]
hermes_relevance: Critical. Hermes invokes via `npx how-to-work@latest` and resolves answerGate.base, answerGate.mode, canonicalUrlBase, doc.* from config — not hardcoded. `check --online` lets Hermes detect engine drift before a batch run.

user_story: As an agent scoping or executing work, I need a single canonical PRD artifact in a predictable on-disk shape so I can read state, append to the ledger, and verify the HTML surface without guessing locations.
expected_behavior: Scaffold with `npx how-to-work@latest new prd <slug>`, producing under doc.prdsDir/<slug>/: index.html, state.json, ledger.jsonl, resources.json, artifacts/, workers/, checks/. Use concise slugs — never prefix with 'canonical-'. Register with `register --all` after creation. [code: how-we-work/SKILL.md:47-60]
hermes_relevance: Hermes reads state.json and ledger.jsonl directly as the machine-readable source of truth — never reconstructs state from chat. The fixed package shape lets Hermes locate state.json via config doc.prdsDir without path discovery.
error_notes: scope/SKILL.md:31 makes three false claims about the scaffold: (1) says the .doc.md source goes 'under doc.sourcesDir' — engine actually writes PRD source to doc.prdsDir/<slug>/index.doc.md; (2) says the scaffold includes ':::questions grill-card stubs at the top' — fresh scaffold has no question block, only :::callout + ## Problem + :::rows + :::resources; (3) says 'Rendering it produces index.html, state.json, ledger.jsonl, resources.json' — state.json and ledger.jsonl come from `htw new`, not render, and resources.json is never created by any command. Additionally, how-we-work/SKILL.md:47-56 lists resources.json, artifacts/, workers/, and checks/ as part of the 'Default package' — none of these four items are created by `htw new` or `htw render`. | FIXED P3

user_story: As an agent tracking or reporting PRD progress, I need a canonical lifecycle sequence so I can set/read stage without ambiguity and render it consistently.
expected_behavior: Progress is the current stage in: Working doc -> Draft PRD -> Ready for approval -> Approved -> In execution -> Done. Stage is sourced from config doc.stagesPath or the engine's built-in 6-stage sequence. Rendered as a monochrome segmented stage bar (or ring in tight spaces). Percent-complete is deprecated and at most secondary. [code: how-we-work/SKILL.md:38-45,80-88]
hermes_relevance: Hermes reads state.json `stage` as the authoritative progress signal. Flipping a stage (e.g. Approved -> In execution at send-it) means writing state.json stage/phase, .doc.md frontmatter, a ledger.jsonl event, and re-rendered HTML in one atomic pass.

feature: Grill: batch independent questions, one-at-a-time for dependent ones
user_story: As a human answering grill questions, I want to respond to all independent forks in one pass but be walked through dependent decisions sequentially so I'm never asked a meaningless question before its dependency resolves.
expected_behavior: When multiple questions do not depend on each other, present them together as sibling qcard articles in one qstack in a single response. When a question's answer changes what follows, send only that one card and wait for the answer before presenting the next. [code: grill/SKILL.md:30-32; how-we-work/SKILL.md:103-104]
hermes_relevance: When Hermes is the questioner, it must enforce this sequencing itself — not batch all questions up front. Hermes receives answers via the custom (onAnswer) path and parses which cards are newly resolved before continuing with the next dependent card or closing.

feature: Grill: stable card IDs and no reply-shorthand line
user_story: As an agent parsing grill answers, I need each question to carry a stable ID so I can correlate an answer to the exact fork it resolves, without ambiguity across re-grill iterations.
expected_behavior: Every question card carries a stable ID Q1, Q2, Q3 ... in data-qid. The recommendation section ends with the actual recommendation text — never a 'Reply X to accept / X <custom>' line. The interactive card and Copy-answers button generate the shorthand; the agent must not add it manually. [code: grill/SKILL.md:33-35; how-we-work/SKILL.md:104-105]
hermes_relevance: Hermes parses submitted payloads keyed by data-qid. Stable IDs guarantee a re-grill (after a custom/idk answer) does not confuse Q2-round-2 with Q2-round-1. Absent reply-shorthand keeps the payload clean for machine parsing.

feature: Start the answer gate immediately after posting questions
user_story: As an agent that just posted grill cards, I must open the answer gate right away so the human can click Submit in the doc and I receive structured answers directly, with no copy-paste handoff.
expected_behavior: After rendering question cards, run `npx how-to-work@latest grill ask --doc <slug> --base <answerGate.base>` (foreground when the agent can block, background otherwise). This opens the ask so the question section lights up 'the agent is waiting for your answers' and polls the gate until Submit, then delivers structured answers to the waiting process. [code: grill/SKILL.md:54-62; how-we-work/SKILL.md:106]
hermes_relevance: For Hermes the gate is the primary delivery path. In the custom (onAnswer) mode the host wires a callback that delivers answers directly to the thread — Hermes must not wait for chat/clipboard. If the agent stalls before starting the gate, submitted answers are stranded in the store and must be recovered via GET <answerGate.base>/result?key=<docKey>.

user_story: As an agent, I need to know how the answer gate behaves in the current repo mode so I handle delivery correctly and don't treat a missing live gate as an error.
expected_behavior: Read answerGate.mode before deciding behavior. 'local': file-backed loopback store; stranded answers recoverable via GET <answerGate.base>/result?key=<docKey>. 'hermes' (the engine enum value is 'custom'): host-private onAnswer; the public engine ships no such delivery so this mode is a no-op without the host overlay. 'none': no server; Copy-answers button is the equivalent; the gate command is a safe no-op, not a bug. [code: grill/SKILL.md:64-68; how-we-work/SKILL.md:106]
hermes_relevance: The custom/hermes mode is the designed Hermes delivery path: when the host wires onAnswer, Hermes receives answers as a push event into the running thread rather than by polling. Hermes detects mode from config before invoking grill ask and handles all three without hardcoding. (Skill copy says 'hermes'; engine schema validates 'custom'.)
error_notes: All three relevant SKILL.md files document answerGate.mode as 'none | local | hermes': grill/SKILL.md:24 writes 'none | local | hermes'; grill/SKILL.md:67 describes the 'hermes' mode; how-we-work/SKILL.md:106 says 'in local/hermes mode'; doc/SKILL.md:49 lists 'none | local | hermes'. The engine validates only ['none', 'local', 'custom'] (check.mjs:58). Setting mode='hermes' causes htw check to exit 1 with 'answerGate.mode "hermes" is not one of none, local, custom'. An agent that follows the skills and writes mode='hermes' breaks htw check. The feature spec notes '(Skill copy says hermes; engine schema validates custom)' but the SKILL.md files themselves never explain that 'hermes' is not a valid engine enum value. | FIXED P3

feature: Custom or 'idk' answer is not a close — refine and re-grill
user_story: As an agent processing an ambiguous or deferred grill answer, I must not close the card and move on; I must fold the intent into a sharper recommendation and re-present just that card.
expected_behavior: On a custom answer or 'idk', do NOT promote the card to :::decisions. Fold the submitted intent into a tightened recommendation, pick the minimal option that makes the next action obvious, and re-present that one card. Move a card into :::decisions (with [Decided <date>]) only once genuinely settled. [code: grill/SKILL.md:71-73; how-we-work/SKILL.md:109-110]
hermes_relevance: Hermes must not treat a non-explicit answer as decision confirmation. The re-grill loop may run multiple times for one card; Hermes preserves the stable Q1 ID across iterations and writes the decision to state.json + ledger.jsonl only once the card is genuinely closed.

feature: Update all visible surfaces in the same pass after answers
user_story: As a human who just submitted grill answers, I expect to reload the doc and see no stale 'open' badges, no outdated next actions, and a fully consistent HTML surface — not just an updated state.json.
expected_behavior: In one atomic pass: update .doc.md source, state.json, ledger.jsonl, re-render to index.html, re-verify the served URL returns 200. Rendered HTML must never lag the latest decision. Resolved question cards move from the top queue into :::decisions; ledger gets an appended event. [code: how-we-work/SKILL.md:107-110; grill/SKILL.md:74-75]
hermes_relevance: Processing a delivered answer batch, Hermes writes all 4-5 artifacts (source + state + ledger + rendered HTML + catalog) together before replying. A partial update (only state.json) leaves the surface inconsistent and confuses a later Hermes session reading the HTML as ground truth.

feature: Feedback on a rendered surface edits the source, never the HTML
user_story: As an agent receiving a screenshot or inline comment about the rendered PRD page, I must edit the .doc.md source and re-render rather than patching the output HTML directly.
expected_behavior: Any feedback referencing a rendered page (screenshot, comment, 'the card still says X') triggers: edit .doc.md (+ state.json / ledger.jsonl as needed) -> re-render -> re-verify. The rendered index.html is a generated artifact and must never be hand-edited. [code: how-we-work/SKILL.md:110-111; grill/SKILL.md:74]
hermes_relevance: Hermes must never shortcut by patching rendered HTML (e.g. to fix a label) — the engine owns rendering and would overwrite it on the next render. Treat index.html as read-only output; always write to source first.

feature: /scope stops at a grilled draft — no execution, no self-answering
user_story: As a human invoking /scope, I expect the agent to deliver a draft PRD with grill cards open and then stop — not begin worktree setup, not answer its own questions, not execute any work.
expected_behavior: The scope skill runs exactly: read intent -> route repo -> scaffold package -> draft PRD tab -> post grill cards -> start answer gate -> set lifecycle to Draft PRD (Scoping) -> register + verify -> closeout. It does NOT start send-it, execution, worktrees, or any maker/checker loop, and does NOT answer its own grill questions. It stops at a grilled draft awaiting answers. [code: scope/SKILL.md:39-47]
hermes_relevance: Hermes invoking /scope respects the same boundary: after delivering the scoped draft and starting the gate, it parks and waits for the onAnswer callback (or polls the gate). It must not speculatively execute work or fill in answers on the author's behalf.
error_notes: scope/SKILL.md step 6 correctly sets lifecycle to 'Draft PRD (Scoping)' (stage 2 in the 6-stage sequence). However, the 'Where /scope stops' section (line 40) says '/scope ends at a grilled draft awaiting answers — Ready-for-approval, not approved, not executed.' The phrase 'Ready-for-approval' shares its name with the actual lifecycle stage 3 ('Ready for approval') while step 6 explicitly sets stage to 'Draft PRD' (stage 2). An agent reading the 'Where /scope stops' section could interpret it as the lifecycle stage 'Ready for approval' rather than as a semantic description of being ready for the human to review grill answers. This is an ambiguous label, not a factual error about engine behavior. | FIXED P3

user_story: As a human saying 'send it', I expect the agent to verify the PRD is complete before dispatching any work, so execution never starts against an under-specified plan.
expected_behavior: Do not start send-it until the PRD has: scope and non-goals, acceptance criteria, current state, reverse-chron ledger, resource tree, and approval for meaningful side effects. If any precondition is missing, block and surface the gap. [code: how-we-work/SKILL.md:147-153]
hermes_relevance: On a 'send it' signal (chat or tool call), Hermes runs this precondition check against state.json before dispatching any worker/subagent. A missing acceptance-criteria field in state.json is a hard stop, not a warning.

feature: Send-it start ritual (goal prompt + lifecycle flip + surfaces + banner)
user_story: As an agent starting a send-it run, I need a structured ritual before dispatching any work so any future cold-started thread — including a Hermes session waking from a watchdog — can re-enter the loop without reconstructing intent from chat.
expected_behavior: The moment send-it starts, in order: (1) author + persist the goal prompt to state.json execution.goalPrompt and the ticket resumePrompt (objective + done-condition + next move + held gates + on-wake/recovery line); (2) flip lifecycle stage to 'In execution' on all surfaces atomically (.doc.md frontmatter, state.json, ledger.jsonl event, re-rendered HTML, verified served URL); (3) update the work ticket to in-progress/agent if workOsLinking configured; (4) surface an execution banner at the top of the doc naming worktree path, branch, base commit, current lane/step, held-for-author items; mirror into state.json.execution. [code: how-we-work/SKILL.md:155-162]
hermes_relevance: The goal prompt's on-wake/recovery line is written for Hermes cold-start re-entry: when a watchdog wakes the thread, Hermes reads state.json execution.goalPrompt to reconstruct the loop objective without parsing chat. The ticket resumePrompt is a secondary cross-session fallback.

user_story: As a human who approved a send-it, I expect the agent to drive the work to its done-condition without stopping at every lane boundary to ask permission to continue.
expected_behavior: After the start ritual, the agent drives the goal to the done-condition in the goal prompt without pausing at lane/step boundaries. Only hard stops: held gates (commit/push, prod/external deploys, declared irreversible actions) and genuine blockers. Progress is reflected by updating surfaces as work proceeds, not by stopping for approval. [code: how-we-work/SKILL.md:163-164]
hermes_relevance: Hermes as a send-it driver must not insert conversational check-ins between lanes. It updates state.json and ledger.jsonl at each completed step, re-renders, and continues. The human sees progress via the PRD surface, not via chat turns.

feature: Worktree discipline — ephemeral isolation for substantial work
user_story: As an agent executing a long-running or potentially-breaking change, I need an isolated ephemeral git worktree so concurrent uncommitted work on main is never entangled with my changes.
expected_behavior: For substantial/breaking work (>~a minute to implement, or could break the running app): create an ephemeral worktree via git worktree add (or config executionEnv.worktreeUp), branching off committed main HEAD. Install deps, provision an isolated backend when declared (executionEnv.isolatedBackend). Build, typecheck, run acceptance tests inside the worktree. Commit in the worktree, merge to main, rebuild, tear down with executionEnv.worktreeDown. Small/quick/low-risk changes ride main directly. [code: how-we-work/SKILL.md:124-145]
hermes_relevance: Hermes surfaces the worktree URL (devUrlBase + worktree port) to the human at the top of the doc as soon as a preview is available, and persists worktree/branch/baseCommit into state.json.execution so a recovering session knows where the work lives.

feature: PRD review and approval order — dependency-first
user_story: As an agent reviewing multiple PRDs, I need to resolve them dependency-first so I never approve a downstream implementation before the enabling primitive it depends on is proven.
expected_behavior: Review in dependency order: (1) upstream/platform primitives first (check if a linked PR or platform feature obsoletes a custom design); (2) the enabling PRD (e.g. local sync or safe dogfood lane); (3) the system PRD after the primitive is proven; (4) lower-level defaults/tooling PRDs last. When a PRD 'needs to be reframed after checkout': checkout worktree -> verify PR -> reframe PRD -> update state/ledger -> re-render -> verify served link. [code: how-we-work/SKILL.md:65-75]
hermes_relevance: Reviewing a queue of PRDs, Hermes builds the dependency graph from state.json dependencies (or infers from content) and sequences review accordingly — not by submission order or recency.

feature: Required surfaces — PRD/Progress/Ledger tabs + stage bar + docs index
user_story: As a human monitoring a PRD, I expect exactly three tabs (PRD, Progress, Ledger), a stage bar showing lifecycle position, and a docs index that surfaces active work ahead of scoping docs.
expected_behavior: The engine emits exactly PRD, Progress, Ledger tabs (1/2/3 shortcuts). The design system (warm neutral background, compact header, chips, no blue focus ring, left-rail dot ledger timeline, stage bar) is engine-owned — never generate bespoke per-doc themes. The docs index is regenerated with `npx how-to-work@latest index`; active work surfaces ahead of scoping docs. [code: how-we-work/SKILL.md:77-88]
hermes_relevance: Hermes must not generate ad-hoc HTML layouts — all PRD HTML comes from the engine's render pipeline so the design system stays consistent. The docs index is the Hermes-readable catalog of live PRD state; consult it to understand what is active before starting a new PRD.

user_story: As an agent verifying a rendered PRD, I need to check structural correctness so I catch regressions before handing the URL to the author.
expected_behavior:
Rendered HTML must: use
with a single
containing sibling
— never nested qcards; strip the first H1 from body (shell header owns the title); use
and
; use a left-rail dot timeline (.timeline + .event), not card-style ledger articles. Verify with `npx how-to-work@latest verify`: no `
`, no `

` after the questions block, all 3 tab buttons exist, served URL returns 200 text/html. [code: how-we-work/SKILL.md:89-98]
hermes_relevance: Hermes runs `npx how-to-work@latest verify` after every render and parses its exit code + output as machine-readable pass/fail. A non-zero exit blocks delivery of the PRD URL to the author.
error_notes: grill/SKILL.md:42-46 documents the question card outer wrapper as '<section class="questions-block">' but the engine emits '<div class="qreview" data-qstack>' (different tag AND class). how-we-work/SKILL.md:95 says 'Use explicit containers: <section class="questions-block">, <section class="prd-body">, <section id="progress">, and <section id="ledger">.' Neither <section class="questions-block"> nor <section class="prd-body"> appear anywhere in the engine output. The engine only emits <section id="progress"> and <section id="ledger"> correctly. An agent doing structural verification by checking for questions-block or prd-body class names — as the skill instructs — would always fail to find them. | FIXED P3

user_story: As a human or downstream agent reading the end of a PRD/workloop response, I expect a structured summary block with explicit fields so I can parse outcome, artifacts, validation, and next action without reading the full response.
expected_behavior: Every PRD/workloop response ends with an Outcome block: PRD: <path-or-url>; Doc: <path-or-url-or-none>; Files changed:; Validation:; Known risks:; Next exact prompt/action:. The PRD field is a direct URL (canonicalUrlBase-based) or filesystem path. If a PRD was created as Markdown first, the canonical HTML surface must exist before closeout — Markdown-only PRDs are insufficient by default. [code: how-we-work/SKILL.md:196-209]
hermes_relevance: The Outcome block is machine-parseable: downstream Hermes agents/watchdogs extract the PRD URL, validation status, and next action without interpreting prose. The 'Next exact prompt/action' field gives Hermes the literal next invocation, enabling chained autonomous runs.

feature: Send-it watchdog: proof = re-entry in originating thread
user_story: As an agent setting up a watchdog for a long-running send-it, I need verified proof the watchdog can actually wake my thread — not just that the cron job was created.
expected_behavior: Verify end-to-end: (1) create a one-shot no-agent watchdog delivering to the origin thread with a unique sentinel (e.g. WATCHDOG_WAKEBACK_OK); (2) record job id, scheduled time, sentinel; (3) wait for the sentinel to re-enter the assistant thread (the user seeing it elsewhere is NOT the success condition); (4) if it doesn't arrive, inspect watchdog state (last run, status, delivery error, next run); (5) keep a durable fallback — watchdog writes events to state/ledger; the surface reads from state. Healthy checks stay silent; only stale/failed/blocked/retried/completed runs wake the thread. [code: how-we-work/SKILL.md:184-192]
hermes_relevance: Hermes uses thread re-entry as the primary wake mechanism; the watchdog targets the exact thread ID from state.json.execution.threadId. The durable state/ledger fallback is critical: if re-entry fails, a freshly woken session reads ledger.jsonl to reconstruct what the watchdog would have reported.

feature: Serving and repo routing — URL first, workflow home for cross-cutting docs
user_story: As an author on a different device, I need every reply that creates/updates a doc to lead with the browser-openable URL — not a filesystem path — so I can open it from any machine.
expected_behavior: Docs are automatically served. Final replies put the clickable web URL first (canonicalUrlBase + catalog path, or devUrlBase when no public host is configured), local path second. Cross-repo / personal / agent-workflow docs route to the configured workflow home (not the current cwd). If no served URL exists, say so explicitly and treat serving as a blocker. [code: how-we-work/SKILL.md:175-182; scope/SKILL.md:36-37]
hermes_relevance: Hermes includes the URL in its structured reply so the human can tap it from a mobile device. It resolves canonicalUrlBase or devUrlBase from config — not memory — and must not emit a localhost URL when a Tailscale/public URL is configured.

feature: doc skill: engine owns rendering — author semantic source only
user_story: As an agent producing a doc artifact, I must author .doc.md semantic source and invoke the engine's render pipeline rather than hand-writing HTML, so the doc inherits the bundled theme and component vocabulary automatically.
expected_behavior: Use `npx how-to-work@latest new <kind> <slug>` to scaffold, render to emit self-contained HTML, register --all to update the catalog, index to regenerate the dashboard, verify to validate, contract to print the authoring contract. Never hand-write a theme, layout, or boilerplate the engine owns. Apply doc.themeFile when configured; otherwise use the bundled default theme. [code: doc/SKILL.md:55-64]
hermes_relevance: Hermes invokes engine CLI commands as subprocess steps, not raw HTML strings, keeping output deterministic and verifiable by `verify`. Engine CLI exit codes are the machine-readable pass/fail signal Hermes uses for verification.

user_story: As an agent producing a doc that changes how future agents understand the repo, I must classify each conclusion and promote lasting findings to canonical Markdown so they are not lost in a dated HTML artifact.

feature: npx github:aneym/how-to-work invocation — always-latest, no install
user_story: As a Hermes agent driving the workflow kit, I can invoke the engine at any time from any machine or ephemeral context without a prior npm install or version pin, guaranteed to run the latest published version.
expected_behavior: `npx github:aneym/how-to-work <command>` fetches and executes HEAD of the public GitHub repo. The `bin` field in package.json maps to ./bin/htw.mjs so npx resolves the binary automatically. No lockfile, no explicit install, no version mismatch between sessions. [code: package.json:7-8; README.md:31-36]
hermes_relevance: Hermes invokes the engine from a skill tool call or shell step with no setup beyond Node >= 18. The always-latest guarantee means Hermes never holds a stale binary. Skills mandate `npx how-to-work@latest` in every code block — Hermes follows the same form.
error_notes: The feature's `hermes_relevance` says 'Skills mandate `npx how-to-work@latest` in every code block — Hermes follows the same form.' All 4 skills (how-we-work: 7 occurrences, grill: 2, scope: 4, doc: 6 — 19 total) instruct Hermes to run `npx how-to-work@latest <cmd>`. But the package 'how-to-work' is NOT published on npm (confirmed: `npm view how-to-work` returns HTTP 404). The `npx how-to-work@latest` form fails at the package-resolution step before any binary is ever invoked. The README correctly documents the alternative `npx github:aneym/how-to-work` form (GitHub HEAD) which would work, but the skills — described by this feature as 'the canonical spec for agent behavior' — give the broken npm form exclusively. The engine's own advisory output from `htw check` also emits the broken form: 'Stamp a config for staleness tracking: npx how-to-work@latest init'. The bin field (`htw` -> `bin/htw.mjs`) is correctly declared; the fault is the missing npm publish, not the bin mapping. | FIXED P3

Machine output (answer blocks, --no-wait JSON, --version string, init confirmation) goes to process.stdout.write(...). The answer envelope uses ===HWQ-ANSWERS-BEGIN===/===HWQ-ANSWERS-END=== on their own lines for a simple string split. -v/--version is a bare semver + newline. [code: src/commands/grill.mjs:136-143,216-223; bin/htw.mjs:97-99,130-132]
hermes_relevance: Hermes parses tool output programmatically. The stdout/stderr contract lets Hermes capture stdout alone (shell redirection or tool output capture) and receive only the structured payload, not noise.

feature: Deterministic exit codes for all command outcomes
user_story: As a Hermes agent checking whether a CLI step succeeded, I can branch on the process exit code without parsing output text, because every outcome maps to a fixed, documented integer.
expected_behavior: Exit 0 = success (answers received, init written, check passed, render done). Exit 1 = config error / guard failure (check drift, init refused overwrite, serve refused non-loopback). Exit 2 = timeout (grill wait exceeded --timeout). Exit 3 = gate unreachable without --stdin-fallback. Exit 64 = usage error (missing required arg). All paths call process.exit(<code>) explicitly; no path exits via unhandled rejection without a stderr error line. [code: bin/htw.mjs:91-99; src/commands/grill.mjs:174,214,254-260; src/commands/check.mjs:188; src/commands/serve.mjs:98-99]

feature: Skills are SKILL.md files shipped inside the package
user_story: As a Hermes agent with access to the how-to-work package, I can load the how-we-work, grill, scope, and doc skills from the package's skills/ directory without a separate download or registry lookup.

Each carries YAML frontmatter with name, description, version, minEngine, metadata.tags. minEngine declares 'how-to-work >= 0.1.0 (Node >= 18)' so a skill installer can gate on the running engine version before loading. [code: package.json:13; skills/*/SKILL.md:1-8]
hermes_relevance: A Hermes tap installer locates and registers the skills from the installed package path without a separate registry. The minEngine frontmatter is a machine-checkable compatibility predicate. error_notes: The package structure is correct (skills in files array, all 4 SKILL.md files present with correct YAML frontmatter including minEngine). However, the skills document `answerGate.mode` as accepting `none | local | hermes` as valid values (grill/SKILL.md line 24; how-we-work/SKILL.md references 'local/hermes mode' together). But the engine's schema in src/commands/check.mjs line 58 defines `answerGateModes: ["none", "local", "custom"]` — 'hermes' is not an accepted value. If an agent follows the skills (which the feature calls 'the canonical spec for agent behavior') and writes `answerGate.mode: "hermes"` in a repo config, `htw check` will exit 1 with the error 'answerGate.mode "hermes" is not one of none, local, custom'. The skills are not the canonical spec in this case — the engine's validator contradicts them on a key enum value. Additionally, all 19 skill code blocks reference `npx how-to-work@latest` (not on npm) which compounds F094. | FIXED P3

When such a bare line (e.g. 'Related Docs') precedes indented children, the children silently attach to the PREVIOUS parent; if that parent has an href, the children are then silently lost in rendering (since renderResources only renders href OR children, not both). A bare label at the start of a block with children causes the first child to become a top-level row and subsequent children to be silently dropped. No error is emitted. The stated behaviors (linked row, unlinked parent with ::, bare label with ::) all work correctly. | FIXED P3
